entries.title: Section 80C
entries.body: Deduction up to ₹1,50,000 per financial year for specified investments/payments: EPF/VPF, PPF, ELSS mutual funds, life insurance premium (subject to 10% sum-assured cap), NSC, 5-year tax-saver FD, Sukanya Samriddhi, principal repayment on home loan, tuition fees for up to 2 children, stamp duty & registration on house purchase. Combined cap with 80CCC and 80CCD(1) is ₹1,50,000 (Section 80CCE). Only available under the old tax regime.

entries.title: Section 80CCD(1B)
entries.body: Additional deduction up to ₹50,000 for a self-contribution to NPS Tier-I, over and above the ₹1.5L limit of 80CCE. Only available under the old regime.

entries.title: Section 80CCD(2)
entries.body: Deduction for the employer's contribution to NPS Tier-I. Up to 10% of salary (Basic+DA) for private employees; up to 14% for central government employees. Available under BOTH old and new regimes — one of the few Chapter VI-A deductions retained in the new regime.

entries.title: Section 80D
entries.body: Deduction for medical-insurance premium and preventive health check-up. Self + spouse + children: up to ₹25,000 (₹50,000 if any insured person is 60+). Parents: additional ₹25,000 (₹50,000 if senior). Preventive health check-up sub-limit: ₹5,000 within the overall cap. For senior citizens without insurance, actual medical expenditure up to ₹50,000 is allowed. Only under old regime.

entries.title: Section 80TTA
entries.body: Deduction up to ₹10,000 on interest from savings bank accounts with a bank, co-operative bank or post office. Available only under the old regime. Senior citizens claim ₹50,000 under 80TTB instead (which also covers FD interest).

entries.title: Section 80G
entries.body: Deduction for donations to eligible charities/funds. Some (like PM Relief Fund) qualify for 100% without ceiling; others for 50% with or without a 10%-of-adjusted-gross-income ceiling. Cash donations above ₹2,000 are NOT eligible — use banking channels. Only under the old regime.

entries.title: Section 139(1)
entries.body: Section 139(1) is the regular provision under which an original return of income is filed within the due date (usually 31 July for individuals not subject to audit; 31 October for audit cases). Most first-time filings use 139(1).

entries.title: Section 139(4)
entries.body: Section 139(4) allows a belated return to be filed after the 139(1) due date, up to 31 December of the assessment year. Late-filing fee u/s 234F applies (₹1,000 if income ≤ ₹5L, else ₹5,000). Losses (other than unabsorbed depreciation and house-property loss) cannot be carried forward in a belated return.

entries.title: Section 139(5)
entries.body: Section 139(5) allows a taxpayer to revise a previously filed return to correct omissions or wrong statements, up to 31 December of the assessment year or before completion of assessment, whichever is earlier. The original acknowledgment number is required.

entries.title: Section 139(8A) — ITR-U
entries.body: Section 139(8A) introduced the updated return (ITR-U) — filed within 24 months (proposed to be extended to 48 months) from the end of the assessment year. Additional tax of 25% or 50% (depending on timing) applies over the base tax and interest. Cannot be used to claim a refund or reduce tax liability; only to declare additional income.

entries.title: Section 234F — late filing fee
entries.body: Section 234F levies a fee for filing the return after the due date: ₹5,000 generally, reduced to ₹1,000 if total income does not exceed ₹5,00,000. The fee is in addition to interest u/s 234A/B/C on unpaid tax.
